sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Cardiac arrest is a medical emergency that occurs when the heart suddenly stops beating effectively, leading to a cessation of blood flow to the brain and other vital organs. This condition can result in loss of consciousness and, if not treated immediately, can lead to death within minutes.
A coordinated, well-rehearsed resuscitation attempt, with a team leader and all team members effectively delivering specific roles, will improve the effectiveness of CPR and the overall resuscitation.
Four people with a minimum skillset of basic life support (BLS) and defibrillation should attend patients in cardiac arrest where possible. This should include at least one registered healthcare professional with advanced life support (ALS) competencies. One person with clinical decision making and leadership skills should form part of the team.
Some members may not be part of the emergency response, for example, a first aider with CPR training.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Unresponsive with no signs of life
- Inadequate or no respirations
- No palpable pulses
- As soon as Cardiac Arrest is established, commence resuscitation. If in doubt, start resuscitative efforts until facts are established
- If obvious death has occurred or injuries incompatible with life, cease resuscitation efforts

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Depending on the cause, ensure scene safety. Responder safety is paramount
- Move patient if in confined space that prohibits initiation of rescue efforts
- Take over compressions from bystanders on arrival
- If you have no back up, stick to BLS. Do not compromise compressions, oxygenation and defibrillation for ALS skills
- Interventions such as vascular access, medications or advanced airway management are not a priority and should only occur prior to the arrival of further clinicians if they do not interrupt high quality CPR
- Once back up has arrived, work as a team, communicate well and establish a ‘hands- off’ overview position once advanced skills have been administered
- Minimise interruptions in chest compressions
- Pulse checks should be performed only when there is evidence of an organised rhythm

- Pulse checks should be performed only when there is evidence of an organised rhythm
- Early preparation for transport is de-emphasised in most cases, except where there are compelling circumstances

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey, especially danger checks
- Apply defibrillator (Zoll X Series or Corpuls) and utilise manual mode if trained:
  - 200J if using a biphasic defibrillator
  - 360J if using a monophasic defibrillator
- If using an AED, follow device prompts
- Continue CPR at ratio of 30 compressions – 2 breaths
- Assess rhythm every 2 minutes; defibrillate when indicated with minimal pause

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- A supraglottic airway should be the first-line adjunct in most cases unless contraindicated
  - Do not attempt endotracheal intubation where a supraglottic device is adequate
- Without compromising high-performance CPR:
  - Gain IV/IO access

**Shockable rhythm (VF / Pulseless VT):**
  - Adrenaline after the second shock, then every second loop
  - Amiodarone after a total of 3 shocks (including pre-arrival AED shocks) if VF/pVT does not respond to defibrillation

**Non-shockable rhythm (Asystole / PEA):**
  - Adrenaline as soon as IV/IO access is established, then every second loop
  - Do not administer Amiodarone

- Consider alternate pad positioning (anterior/posterior) if still in VF/VT after 3 shocks and anti-arrhythmic medication
- Consider magnesium for torsades de pointes
- Consider reversible causes. Assess for all whilst performing high quality CPR:

**The Hs:**
- Hypovolaemia
- Hypoxia
- Hypo/Hyperkalaemia
- Hypo/Hyperthermia
- Hypoglycaemia

**The Ts:**
- Toxins
- Thrombosis
- Tamponade
- Tension Pneumothorax

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - The treatment for cardiac arrest patients revolves around core requirements; excellent quality chest compressions that are never interrupted unnecessarily, oxygenation and timely defibrillation
